steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers concluded that, independent of avian order, the prevalence of deceptive antipredator displays is mediated by environmental variations associated with absolute latitude. To support this, the correct finding must show that prevalence changes as latitude changes — a direct latitude–prevalence correlation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Charadriiformes independent of latitude, others only at 0°–30°': This contradicts the conclusion by showing the trait is not latitude-dependent in one order and is restricted to low latitudes in others, rather than varying with latitude across all orders. ✗
  • Choice B — '~34% prevalence at 0°–30°, ~60% at 50°–80°': Prevalence nearly doubles as absolute latitude increases from the low band to the high band, directly demonstrating that the trait is mediated by latitude across the orders studied. ✓
  • Choice C — 'Charadriiformes at all latitudes; other orders only 10°–20° above typical': This introduces a species-specific threshold rather than a general latitude effect, and the pattern is not about overall prevalence varying with latitude. ✗
  • Choice D — '54% brood at 50°–80°, but most displayers are at 0°–30°': This shows the opposite of what the conclusion predicts — high-latitude species are not more likely to display the trait — and therefore undermines rather than supports the researchers. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
